key_insight: Raising argon flow from 1600 to 1800 L/h during air plasma spraying of NiCrFeBSiC coatings produced thicker, denser, and more wear-resistant coatings.
engineering_relevance: This suggests argon flow is a practical process lever for tuning plasma-sprayed coating quality, especially when balancing deposition efficiency, porosity, and tribological performance.
method: Air plasma sprayed self-fluxing NiCrFeBSiC coatings at two argon flow rates: 1600 and 1800 L/h.
Compared the resulting microstructure, microhardness, elastic modulus, adhesion, and wear behavior.
Used ball-on-disk tribology and scratch testing to assess performance.
evidence_signal: At 1800 L/h, coating thickness increased about twofold, porosity decreased about twofold, and the friction coefficient dropped to roughly 0.107, with microhardness measured at 1015.8 ± 81.5 HV and elastic modulus at 203.9 ± 5.4 GPa.
watch_item: The study compares only two argon flow settings, so the optimal operating window and scalability to other spray conditions remain uncertain.
paper_title: EFFECT OF ARGON FLOW RATE DURING AIR PLASMA SPRAYING ON THE STRUCTURE AND PROPERTIES OF NICRFEBSIC COATINGS
